questions.question: Why use sacrebleu instead of rolling your own BLEU?
options: It normalizes tokenization so scores are comparable across papers and runs
questions.explanation: sacrebleu freezes tokenization — without it, different tokenizers produce incomparable BLEU numbers, a common source of misleading benchmark comparisons.

questions.question: Which NLLB-specific setting controls the target language during decoding?
options: forced_bos_token_id set to the target language code's token id
questions.explanation: NLLB forces the first decoded token to a target-language code via forced_bos_token_id; without it the model may decode into any language (off-target generation).

questions.question: Which metric family is the 2026 default for production MT quality where labeled data exists?
options: Learned metrics such as COMET (and BERTScore/BLEURT) trained on human judgment
questions.explanation: Learned metrics like COMET correlate more strongly with human judgment than heuristic metrics like BLEU or chrF alone, making them the 2026 production standard.

questions.question: When does chrF tend to be more informative than BLEU?
options: For morphologically rich languages where character-level matches catch inflectional variants BLEU misses
questions.explanation: Character F-score captures partial morphological matches — inflected forms, compound splits — that word-level BLEU counts as completely wrong.

questions.question: Why does fine-tuning on a few thousand high-quality domain pairs often beat much larger noisy web data?
options: Web data is illegal to use
Quality and domain match dominate volume; noisy parallel data introduces drift and hallucination
questions.explanation: Clean domain-aligned pairs are the largest production quality lever; noisy web-scraped data introduces mistranslation patterns and hallucination that corrupt fine-tuning.

questions.question: When is an LLM (e.g. GPT-4) likely to outperform a specialized MT model in 2026?
options: Idiomatic content, long context, stylistic adaptation via prompting, or content requiring tone control
Small-language pairs with millions of parallel sentences
questions.explanation: LLMs win on idiomatic phrasing, long-context coherence, and style-controlled translation; specialized MT models win on throughput, latency, and cost.
